coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent sensor for UO2 2+ detection based on Ag+-modified gold nanoclusters hybrid via photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Wen-Juan
Nan, Hua-Rui
Peng, Hong-Bo
Wang, You-Qun
Dong, Zhi-Min
Zhang, Zhi-Bin
Cao, Xiao-Hong
Liu, Yun-Hai
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we prepared a new ratiometric fluorescent sensor by first encapsulating blue-fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) with SiO2, coupling gold nanoclusters (GSH-AuNCs) to the surface, and finally anchoring a layer of Ag+. The presence of UO2 2+ caused fluorescence quenching of the ratiometric fluorescent probe when used in aqueous solutions. Furthermore, HOMO–LUMO orbital analysis suggested that the quenching mechanism was mainly attributed to photoinduced electron transfer (PET) between B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ and UO2 2+. Under optimal conditions, the ratiometric fluorescent probes exhibited excellent selectivity and detected UO2 2+ at 37 nmol/L. This new “on–off” ratiometric fluorescent sensor exhibits promising potential for convenient monitoring of UO2 2+ levels with ideal recovery in real-world water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent sensing
Gold nanoclusters
Uranyl ions
Photoinduced electron transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of ratiometric fluorescent sensor system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) B-CDs@SiO2, (b) B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs and their photos under natural light (inset, top) or 365 nm UV lamp (inset, bottom). (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs, GSH-AuNCs, B-CDs@SiO2 and B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs. (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs and B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of UO2 2+ and fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+. (b)Dynamic light scattering particle size of GSH-AuNCs before (top) and after (bottom) the addition of UO2 2+. (c) Fluorescence lifetime (660 nm) of B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ before and after the addition of 0.4 μM UO2 2+. (d) Stern-Volmer plots at different temperatures.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of (a) Uranyl nitrate, (b) B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ and their Tauc plot (inset). (c) XPS-VB spectra of Uranyl nitrate (top) and B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ (bottom). (d) PET process between B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ and uranyl nitrate.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the ratiometric fluorescent sensor after adding different concentrations of UO2 2+ ions. (b) Relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio F/F440 and the concentration of UO2 2+ ions and the fitting of the linear regression equation. Inset: photos of ratiometric fluorescent sensor under 365 nm UV lamp. (c) Normalized fluorescence signal ratios after adding different concentrations of interfering ions.
The preparation process of B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ and the schematic diagram of the detection of UO2 2+.
Determination of UO2 2+ in real samples using the ratiometric fluorescent sensor.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *Sea water samples have been filtered three times through a 0.45 mm pore size filter membrane.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent sensor for UO2 2+ detection based on Ag+-modified gold nanoclusters hybrid via photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Nuclear Resources and Environment, East China University of Technology, Nanchang, Jiangxi 330013, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Nuclear Resources and Environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We herein construct a ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on the photoinduced electron transfer for the sensitive detection of uranyl ions in real samples.

In this study, we prepared a new ratiometric fluorescent sensor by first encapsulating blue-fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) with SiO2, coupling gold nanoclusters (GSH-AuNCs) to the surface, and finally anchoring a layer of Ag+. The presence of UO2 2+ caused fluorescence quenching of the ratiometric fluorescent probe when used in aqueous solutions. Furthermore, HOMO–LUMO orbital analysis suggested that the quenching mechanism was mainly attributed to photoinduced electron transfer (PET) between B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ and UO2 2+. Under optimal conditions, the ratiometric fluorescent probes exhibited excellent selectivity and detected UO2 2+ at 37 nmol/L. This new “on–off” ratiometric fluorescent sensor exhibits promising potential for convenient monitoring of UO2 2+ levels with ideal recovery in real-world water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence analysis of heavy metal ions and radioactive ions has attracted increasing attention in recent years owing to its affordability, high sensitivity, facile operation, and portability [1–4]. In contrast to single-emission fluorescent sensors, ratiometric fluorescent probes generally have two fluorescent signals: reference signal and response signal. The existence of both signals can improve the accuracy and sensitivity of the detection method, as it has a self-calibration function and can eliminate the impact of analytical environment (solvent, temperature, instrument, photobleaching, etc.) [5–7]. In addition, more importantly, the clever combination of response signal and reference signal fluorescence colors can visually reflect the concentration changes of the target to be measured, which is very conducive to on-site quantitative determination [8,9]. In recent years, they have been widely used for the detection of heavy metal ions in solutions [10,11]. Yan et al. established a sensing platform for the detection of NO2 by mixing blue-glowing carbon quantum dot (CDs) with red-glowing quantum dot (QDs) [12]. Despite the simplicity and feasibility of this method, the inhomogeneity of the concentration distribution after mixing, as well as the interaction between fluorescent nanoprobes, often leads to poor detection reproducibility. The most common method to solve this issue is by encapsulating fluorescent nanoprobes in different materials, which not only provides strong protection for fluorescence nanoprobes but also enhances their fluorescence performance [13]. Currently, the main encapsulation materials include polymers [14], silicas [15], carbon materials [16,17], and metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) [18].

Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) are widely used in the field of fluorescent sensors because of their large Stokes shift, high molar absorption coefficient, and fluorescence quantum yield [19–22]. However, many factors, including temperature, sulfhydryl compounds, and strong reducing agents, can hinder the stability of AuNCs, thereby limiting their application. Specifically, high temperatures can dislodge ligands from the surface of AuNCs and cause them to aggregate into larger particles [23]. In biological systems, thiol-protected AuNCs can undergo exchange reactions with exogenous thiols, leading to the loss of targeting modifications and alterations in properties (e.g., optical properties and aggregation resistance) [24,25]. Some strong reducing agents, such as NaBH4, can even reduce Au (I) on the surface of AuNCs to Au (0), causing them to lose their coordination ability with the protecting agent.

Silica nanoparticles (SNPs) are frequently used as encapsulation materials for AuNCs owing to their mechanical stability, facile synthesis, biocompatibility, and ease of surface functionalization [26,27]. The core–shell structure formed by SNPs can provide a passivation layer for the inner AuNCs, effectively preventing oxygen or water from entering the interior. Rao et al. [28] encapsulated blue-emitting CDs with SNPs and covalently linked red-emitting QDs to the surface to construct a ratiometric fluorescent system for determining Cu2+ ions with a detection limit of 0.096 μM. Different concentrations of Cu2+ can enable a ratiometric fluorescent system to achieve a continuous change of color from red to blue.

Since its discovery in 1789, uranium has been widely used in military and civilian applications—initially as a porcelain colorant—then as a raw material for nuclear energy and weapons of mass destruction, such as the atomic bomb [29]. In recent years, a worldwide series of nuclear accidents has caused large amounts of uranium-containing wastewater to be discharged into the environment, which has led to uranium contamination in wastewater becoming an issue that cannot be ignored [30]. In an aqueous environment, uranium with high ionic mobility can enter the human body through inhalation, wounds and other pollution channels, causing irreversible harm to the human body [31]. The World Health Organization (WHO) defines the guideline value of uranium in water as 15 ppb [32]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a sensitive and selective method for detecting uranyl ions (UO2 2+).

In this study, we designed a stable, shell-protected novel ratiometric fluorescent probe (B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+) for sensitive detection of uranyl in water, as shown in Scheme 1 . First, blue-fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) were encapsulated into SiO2 nanoparticles using the simple Stöber method. Then, the red-fluorescent gold nanoclusters (GSH-AuNCs) were modified on the surface of the B-CDs@SiO2 nanoparticles via the NHS/EDC coupling reaction. Thereafter, a layer of Ag+ was anchored on the shell of the B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs to enhance the fluorescent performance. The experimental results show that UO2 2+ quenched the fluorescence of B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ via the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism, while the fluorescence color underwent a red-violet-blue change, making the detection facile and more visually perceptible. Notably, the anchoring of Ag+ on the surface of the B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs further improved the response sensitivity and selectivity with an ideal recovery rate for detection in actual water samples.

HAuCl4·3H2O, glutathione, sodium acetate, citric acid, and urea were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chloroacetic acid, dibasic sodium phosphate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, and nitric acid were supplied by Xilong Science Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were obtained from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Uranyl nitrate hexahydrate was supplied by the Xi'an Dingtian Chemical Co., Ltd. (Xian, China). All reagents were used without further purification.

An RF-540 fluorescence spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU, Japan) with a quartz cuvette (10 mm) was used to record the fluorescence spectra at a sweep speed of 600 nm·min−1. The slit widths of both the excitation and emission were 5 nm. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed using a Nova Nano 450 instrument at 200 kV (JEOL, Japan). A TU-1901 UV–Vis spectrometer (China) was used to obtain UV–Vis absorption spectra. zeta potentials of the particles were measured using a Stabino PMX 400 (USA) microelectrophoresis instrument. An FLS 980 Fluorometer (Edinburgh, UK) was used to measure the decay of the fluorescence lifetime.

B-CDs were prepared according to a previously reported method with slight modifications [33]. GSH-AuNCs were synthesized by reducing HAuCl4 with glutathione as a protective and reducing agent [34]. According to the conventional procedure, 1 mL of 10 mM HAuCl4, 0.15 mL of 100 mM glutathione solution, and 3.85 mL of water were mixed well. The mixture was then incubated at 70 °C for 24 h with gentle stirring. The resultant solution was dialyzed for 3 days with a molecular weight of 3000 Da. The obtained GSH-AuNCs solution was stored at 4 °C until further use.

B-CDs@SiO2 were synthesized using a traditional Stöber method with some modifications [35], and B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs were prepared by coupling GSH-AuNCs to B-CDs@SiO2 nanoparticles via NHS/EDC reagents. Specifically, 5 mL of purified B-CDS was mixed with 20 mL of ethanol, 250 μL of APTES, and 1 mL of TEOS and stirred for 1 h. Then, 1 mL of ammonia was added, and stirring was continued for 24 h for silica growth. The obtained B-CDs@SiO2 precipitate was washed three times with alternating ethanol and deionized water and collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm. 10 mg of B-CDs@SiO2 was redispersed in 30 mL of PBS (pH 6.1), and 2 mL of 2 mg/mL NHS/EDC solution was added and stirred for 15 min. 5 mL of GSH-AuNCs solution was then introduced and stirred for 6 h. The resultant B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs were obtained after washing and centrifugation, as described above. Finally, to enhance the fluorescence and mask the interference of Hg2+ [36,37], a layer of Ag+ was anchored on the surface of the GSH-AuNCs.

500 μL of B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ dispersion and 500 μL of 1 mM EDTA solution were dissolved in 2 mL of chloroacetic acid-acetate sodium buffer (pH 3.0). Then, 1 mL of UO2 2+ solution at different concentrations was added, and the emission spectra (λex = 365 nm) were recorded after incubation for 30 s at 25 °C.

The synthesis of the fluorescent probes was characterized, and the results are shown in Fig. 1 . The B-CDs@SiO2 were homogeneous spherical particles with a particle diameter of approximately 150 nm, and the maximum emission wavelength was located at 440 nm, exhibiting blue fluorescence under UV light. Compared to B-CDs (λem = 420 nm), the emission peak position of B-CDs@SiO2 was red-shifted, which was caused by the partial aggregation of B-CDs wrapped in SiO2. In this system, B-CDs were used as the reference fluorescence signal; therefore, their fluorescence red-shift had minimal effect on the detection of UO2 2+. B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs showed two fluorescence peaks corresponding to the emission peaks of B-CDs@SiO2 (λem = 440 nm) and GSH-AuNCs (λem = 627 nm), indicating the successful synthesis of the ratiometric fluorescent probes. When a layer of Ag+ was anchored on the surface of the B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs, the fluorescence intensity of the ratiometric fluorescent probes was significantly enhanced, and the emission peak was red-shifted by 33 nm (Fig. 1d), which is consistent with a previous report [37]. This can be explained by the formation of Ag+–Au+ metallic bonds through the strong metallophilic interaction of the 4d10 center, followed by charge transfer from Ag+ to Au+, which leads to a significant increase in fluorescence intensity. This is very beneficial for improving the sensitivity of turn-off fluorescence sensors. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ reached 70.72% by addition of 4 μM UO2 2+, which was much higher than that of B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs at 49.85%, indicating that B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ has a higher sensitivity to UO2 2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of ratiometric fluorescent sensor system
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental parameters, such as feeding ratios, pH of the solution, and response time during preparation, were optimized to improve the sensitivity and selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescent probes. Fig. S2 shows that the fluorescence intensity ratio of the two probes was close to 1:1 when the feed amounts of B-CDs@SiO2 and GSH-AuNCs were 2 mg and 1 mL, respectively. Meanwhile, Fig. S3 demonstrates the effect that the amount of AgNO3 added had on the fluorescence intensity of ratiometric fluorescent probes. By gradually increasing the amount of AgNO3 in 2 mg B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs solution, the fluorescence intensity first gradually increased and then remained constant. The fluorescence intensity was maximal when the feeding ratio (M/V) was 0.5, and the enhancement was approximately four-fold. According to our previous research study [33], the widest color variation range can be obtained when the ratio of reference-to-response fluorescence intensity is 1:4, which facilitates ratiometric fluorescent visualization. Therefore, we set the amounts of B-CDs@SiO2, GSH-AuNCs, and AgNO3 to 2 mg, 1 mL, and 1 mL, respectively.

The effect of pH on the ratiometric fluorescent probes was investigated in a chloroacetic acid-sodium acetate buffer solution (Fig. S4). Unlike GSH-AuNCs dispersed in an aqueous solution, whose fluorescence intensity decreased significantly with increasing pH, the B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ probes became less sensitive to pH. This can be attributed to the restricted motion of GSH-AuNCs after modification on the surface of SiO2 nanoparticles which caused the aggregation effect to be attenuated. The maximum fluorescence intensity was obtained when the pH was 3.0, thereby indicating that this was the optimal pH for UO2 2+ detection.

Furthermore, the ratiometric fluorescent probes had excellent stability, and the F/F440 ratio decreased by only 4.73% after three months in the chloroacetic acid-sodium acetate buffer solution (pH 3.0) (Fig. S5). The ratiometric fluorescent probes responded quickly to different concentrations of UO2 2+, and response equilibrium was reached at 30 s (Fig. S6). The aforementioned results indicate that B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ probes exhibit potential for the rapid detection of UO2 2+ in water bodies.

In this study, we constructed an “on–off” ratiometric fluorescent sensor to respond to UO2 2+. Generally, the causes of fluorescence quenching include the inner filter effect (IFE), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), aggregated-induced quenching (ACQ), dynamic quenching effect (DQE), and static quenching effect (SQE). The optical properties of UO2 2+ and B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ were investigated to elucidate possible fluorescence quenching mechanisms.

First, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of UO2 2+ showed no obvious absorption peaks in the range of 400–800 nm (Fig. 2 a), and the mechanism of FRET or IFE can be excluded because the possibility of both FRET and IFE occurring depends on the effective overlap between the quencher absorption peak and the fluorophore emission spectrum. Second, the size change and surface charge of B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ and GSH-AuNCs were investigated by dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential measurements. In Fig. S7, the presence of UO2 2+ increased the average hydrodynamic diameter of GSH-AuNCs from 1.5 nm to 14.2 nm, indicating the occurrence of ACQ. In contrast, the average hydrodynamic diameter of B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ did not change significantly, both being 150 nm (Fig. 2b). As seen in Fig. S8, the absolute value of zeta potential of B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ at pH 3.0 was larger than that of GSH-AuNCs. The larger surface electrostatic repulsion made B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ less prone to aggregate, thereby eliminating the possibility of fluorescent quenching due to the ACQ mechanism. Finally, we tested the change in the fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ at 660 nm using steady-state and transient fluorescence spectrometry. As shown in Fig. 2c, the average lifetime at 660 nm, as calculated with Eqn. S1, decreased from 12.94 µs to 10.44 µs after the addition of UO2 2+ (Table. S2). In addition, Stern-Volmer plots showed that the quenching constant increased with the rise of temperature (Fig. 2d), which was the result of the increase in the number of collisions between B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ and UO2 2+ at higher temperature. Both of the above experiments proved that the fluorescence mechanism was a dynamic quenching process. Because the possibility of reduced fluorescence lifetime based on FRET was ruled out, the quenching mechanism of PET was speculated [38]. Further analysis of fluorescence lifetime data, the PET quenching efficiency (EPET %) of 0.4 μM UO2 2+ can be calculated to as 19.32% (Eqn. S3), in good agreement with the total fluorescence quenching efficiency (E%, 21.94%) calculated from Eqn. S2 [39]. This implies that PET is the main non-radiative pathway leading to fluorescence quenching of the ratiometric fluorescent sensing system.

Furthermore, we confirmed the above PET mechanism using HOMO–LUMO orbital studies [40]. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of UO2 2+ and B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNC/Ag+ are shown in Fig. 3 . According to the Tauc plot, the optical bandgaps (Eg ) of both were located at 3.80 eV and 2.71 eV, respectively (Fig. 3a and 3b) [41,42]. The valence band X-ray photoelectron (VB-XPS) spectra revealed the HOMO energies (EHOMO ) of UO2 2+ and B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNC/Ag+, which were estimated to be −2.90 eV and −3.14 eV, respectively (Fig. 3c) [42]. Therefore, according to the formula: ELUMO = EHOMO + Eg
[43], their corresponding LUMO energies (ELUMO ) were calculated to be 0.90 eV and −0.43 eV, respectively. Fig. 3d illustrates the PET process between UO2 2+ and B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ based on the aforementioned results. First, the electrons of B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ were excited to the LUMO under UV light irradiation. Because the HOMO of UO2 2+ is located between the LUMO and HOMO energy levels of B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+, the excited electrons are transferred to the HOMO of UO2 2+ immediately afterward, which effectively prevents the electrons from returning to the HOMO of B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+. Finally, the electrons on the HOMO of UO2 2+ release heat and return to the HOMO of B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+, thereby quenching the fluorescence.

Having identified the quenching mechanism of PET in our ratiometric fluorescent sensor, we focused on its detection performance and explored the sensitivity and selectivity under optimal detection conditions (pH 3.0, 30 s). As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity at 660 nm was gradually quenched with increasing UO2 2+ ion concentration, while that at 440 nm remained nearly constant. A linear relationship was observed over the concentration range of 0.2–1 μmol/L. According to the formula LOD = 3σ/k (where σ is the standard deviation of 10 blank samples and k is slope of regression line) [44], the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 37 nmol/L (Fig. 4b). Moreover, the fluorescence color gradually changed from red to purple, and finally to blue as the concentration of UO2 2+ increased, which could be clearly distinguished with the naked eye (Fig. 4b, inset). Fig. S9 shows a comparison of the analytical performance of the single-signal GSH-AuNC fluorescence sensor under the same experimental conditions. Under the combined effect of ACQ and PET, the single-signal fluorescence sensor demonstrated good fluorescence sensing performance for UO2 2+ with a detection limit of 5.7 nmol/L (S/N = 3). However, the change in fluorescence color and brightness was difficult to detect visually.

The effects of interfering ions such as Zn2+, Cd2+, and Hg2+ on the sensing performance of the ratiometric fluorescent sensor were investigated experimentally. Zn2+ and Cd2+ can produce an AIE effect, leading to an increase in the fluorescence intensity, which interferes with the detection of UO2 2+. Fig. S10 shows that the effect of AIE can be effectively eliminated by loading GSH-AuNCs onto SiO2 nanoparticles [45,46]. In Fig. S10, the anti-interference of B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ to Hg2+ was greatly improved compared with GSH-AuNCs and B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs. This can be explained by the fact that Ag+ can form stronger Ag+–Au+ metallic bonds with Au+ on the surface of GSH-AuNCs, which eliminated the severe fluorescence quenching caused by Hg2+ by competing for the binding sites on the surface of GSH-AuNCs [37]. Additionally, some coexisting ions, including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Cl−, NO3 –, CO3 2–, and PO4 3−, were added to the fluorescence sensing system to verify the selectivity for UO2 2+ detection. As shown in Fig. 4c, up to 100 times the concentration of various coexisting ions did not affect the detection of UO2 2+, indicating the excellent selectivity of the B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ sensor.

The B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ sensor was used to detect UO2 2+ in real samples, and the results are listed in Table 1 . Sample 1 is a concentrated wastewater solution from an evaporation pond. The concentration of relevant ions was measured by ICP-AES and listed in Table S1. The wastewater was diluted in different multiples and the RSD of the five parallel determinations was less than 5.00 %. Samples 2 and 3 were spiked with standard solutions containing different concentrations of UO2 2+, and the recoveries ranged from 95.50 % to 103.00 %. These results indicated that the ratiometric fluorescent sensor exhibits good repeatability and accuracy.

In summary, we successfully constructed an “on-off” ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on a core–shell type of fluorescent probe (B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+) for the measurement of UO2 2+ in aqueous solutions. The experimental and theoretical results indicate that PET is the main mechanism of fluorescence quenching between B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ and UO2 2+. Under optimal detection conditions, B-CDs@SiO2@GSH-AuNCs/Ag+ detected UO2 2+ in solutions with concentrations as low as 37 nmol/L. Moreover, the changes in color from red to purple to blue with increasing UO2 2+ concentration facilitated visual detection. The ratiometric fluorescent probes also exhibited excellent selectivity, especially for Hg2+. The sensor performed well in the detection of UO2 2+ in real-world wastewater samples with ideal recoveries, indicating good potential for rapid monitoring in practical applications.

Wen-Juan Gong wrote the entire draft of the manuscript. Written review and editing of the article by Xiao-Hong Cao. The core conceptual idea and study design were all provided by Zhi-Bin Zhang and Yun-Hai Liu. Hua-Rui Nan and Hong-Bo Peng have performed the preparation, analytical experiments, and the entire data analysis, respectively. You-Qun Wang and Zhi-Min Dong have conducted the characterization analysis of the fluorescent sensor.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22166005, 22076022, U2167223, and 22066003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108725.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile polymerizable surfactant inspired synthesis of fluorescent molecularly imprinted composite sensor via aqueous CdTe quantum dots for highly selective detection of λ-cyhalothrin

Zhou, Facile polymerizable surfactant inspired synthesis of fluorescent molecularly imprinted composite sensor via aqueous CdTe quantum dots for highly selective detection of λ-cyhalothrin, Sens. Actuators B Chem. 224 (2016) 315-324. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.10.048.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthetic ratiometric fluorescent probes for detection of ions

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot synthesis of boron and nitrogen co-doped silicon-carbon dots for fluorescence enhancement and on-site colorimetric detection of dopamine with high selectivity

Xu, One-pot synthesis of boron and nitrogen co-doped silicon-carbon dots for fluorescence enhancement and on-site colorimetric detection of dopamine with high selectivity, Appl. Surf. sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visual detection of Cu2+ in high-copper feed based on a fluorescent derivative of rhodamine B
